summary: Hannes Mühleisen opens DuckCon #5 by reflecting on DuckDB's mission and extraordinary growth. He frames DuckDB's core purpose as replacing fear of data with confidence, comparing the goal to how VLC made video files approachable. He outlines three main use cases: interactive analysis, pipeline components, and creative architectures like mobile devices and satellites. Adoption metrics are staggering — 6 million Python downloads per month, 17 million extension installs, and 600K unique web visitors. He emphasizes DuckDB is not VC-funded, sustained instead by commercial support contracts through DuckDB Labs.
key_points: DuckDB's mission is transforming data from something feared into something approachable, like opening a movie file in VLC
Three distinct use cases emerged: interactive analysis, data pipeline components, and creative architectures (mobile, browser, Lambda, satellites)
Adoption tripled in months — from 2M to 6M Python downloads/month, 5M to 17M extension installs/month
DuckDB 1.0 shipped without catastrophic bugs, and three independently-written books now exist about it
DuckDB Labs is not VC-funded, sustaining itself through commercial support and consulting contracts
money_quotes: People generally fear data. Data sets, they can be unruly. You're gonna have that wrong comma in line 400,000 or something like that.
The tools people generally are using to look at data, or to wrangle with data, they tended to be, I think, hostile is the right word.

We kind of want to get to the same point with this unwieldy collection of CSV files that you have. Data sets should just be harmless. It should be just like a movie file. You just throw it in a VLC and it's gonna be fine.
It's really the first custom built in-process analytical data management system, which is kind of an accident. We didn't really set out to make a new category, but there we are.
You can put DuckDB in a Lambda function. You can run DuckDB in a browser. You can run DuckDB on a satellite in space.
In the past you couldn't just stick a Spark cluster into a pipeline. That doesn't work. But with DuckDB you can just spin up an instance and run some minor transformation.
When we were pushing out 1.0, you have this anxiety — five minutes later somebody will be like, oh by the way, the joints are broken. Didn't happen.
DuckDB is not VC funded. The company that employs the core contributors does fund the whole show by offering commercial support contracts and consulting contracts.
key_insight: DuckDB's core mission is replacing the fear and hostility people experience with data tools by making data as approachable as opening a movie file in VLC.
